column6:
The site has an exisiting track leading to the plot from the main road and Bo township ~1km away.

A stream runs 100m away to the east of the plot, where water/ run-off may be directed. There existing drainage infrastructure surrounding the adjacent industrial buildings.
column7:
~2.5km from the E6 highway, the site has good access and connections to Bodo. The single track road from the main road to the site is currently used for forestry and quarry machinery so would be suitable for construction phases, however this road may need upgrading to account for operational data centre traffic. 

There are no streams running through the site, however the is a river to the south of the side, where run-off water from the site could be directed.
column8:
The site is adjacent to the E8 highway that connects northern Finland to Norway, located ~6km from the town of Skitbotn. 

Exisiting brownfield infrastructure sees this site having suitable access at two points from the main road. However the same access road is used for a holiday park, so additional roads/ security measures would need to be taken to ensure site security. 

Current brownfield infrastructure is sufficient for drainage concerns.
column9:
Set back by ~100m from the main road connecting Honnginsvag town to its airport, the site has good access to the main road, airport and harbour on the island. 

The track leading from the main road to the site is gravel, therefore this will need to be upgraded for traffic of an operational data centre. 

Positioned on a sloping hill, the site doesn't suffer from drainage issues, but will need to be levelled accordingl, with considerations made for both drainage and permafrost regarding the sites Arctic positioning.
column13:
Site is accessed currently from small roads to the North East & South.

The access to the North East would need to be improved and new access implemented from the West over Natural Resources Finland land. The municipality will arrange this. 

The existing access to the South should be improved and used as an emergency access route.

Debtate over who should pay for the Western road connection, us or municipality. Approximate cost is 1mil EUR / km and the distance would be 500m.

There is currently drainage which collects stormwater North of the site and then runs through a channel toward the Western boundary of the site from North to South. This would need addressing.
column14:
Site can be accessed by main road adjacent to the South West of the plot.

No upgrades required to exisiting road infastructure, with access easily granted by close proximity to road 505.

There is an existing road access running through the southern portion of the site connecting road 505 to Lentoasemantie.

No visible hydrological features on the site, with the closest drainage existing on the other side of the 505 from the site.
column15:
Site can be accessed by wide main roads ~140m to the south and west of the plot, although work will need to be conducted to sufficiently connect the main roads to the plot.

Consideration will be needed for the route of access roads regarding the topogaphy, i.e. it might not be suitable for a straight road to access the site from the main road. 

Drainage can be easily managed alongside the levelling of the site, with run-off directed to the north of the site, where there's an existing low-point and drainage.
column16:
The site has potential for great access, with the 663 highway skirting the southern border of the plot. 

An additional access point could be created to the main road adjacent to the north west of the property. 

Due to the large expanse of this land plot, there are many stream and hydological considerations to be made before site plans can be made.
column17:
The currently selected site has multiple access points, however all are single track (tarmac). Nearest main road is ~600m from the site, so investment into the local infrastructure could be necassary. 

With a gentle slope in the direction of the nearby gorge, drainage/ run-off will not be an issue on site.
column18: Current access is a concern with only a small one-track road
column19:
The existing brownfield site has perfect access via main roads at either end of the island, leaving little in terms of required infrastructure upgrades. 

Being flat and adjacent to water bodies, the site has no issue with drainage.
column21:
The existing brownfield site has access via road to the south of the landplot where utilities are currnetly supplied. A further access road is being developed to the North of the plot.


Being flat and adjacent to water bodies, the site has no issue with drainage.
column23:
The site is supplied with good connections to the city of Oulu, ~5km from the city centre. More locally, the site is adjacent to the main E8 and '20' highways. In addition, the proposed data centre site is less than a 20 minute drive from the regional airport, with good connections to Helsinki (1hr flight time). A large port is also located around 10 minute drive from the site, which also poses signigicant opportunities for subsea fibre cables. 

Regarding drainage, the site has multiple drainage ditches surrounding the site. The site is slightly elevated above the surrounding area, demonstrating a low flood risk.
column24:
Brownfield site with excellent access and connections, three access points, north, east and west. Access points into the site are connected to main roads that then quickly merge onto the main costal highway (E4).

The site is also located ~5km from the centre of Skelleftea, therefore very good access/ connections to local ammenities.

Drainage across the brownfield site is already provisioned for. Unless the site is redeveloped very little consideration is needed regarding site drainage.
column25:
The brownfield site has two points of access, one to the north and the other to the west. The western access point connects directly to the E04 main coastal highway running right the way up and down the Swedish coast.

Along the E04 main coastal highway, the nearest large town is Härnösand, 20 minutes drive from the site. 

Regarding drainage, there is currently exsiting infrastructure to manage drainage, however additional ground surveys might be needed due to the age of the brownfield site.
column26:
Site had good access via the main road that leads to the centre of Kalix proper. Kalix having good access to the E4 main highway, with drive times <1hr to Lulea and Kemi (Finland).

In addition, with exisiting small roads/ tracks, it could be possible to easily increase access via other entry points. 

Regarding drainage, currently the site is covered in natural woodland, however there are a number of small streams that would need to be redirected. In addition there is some boggy ground the plot, so hydrological engineers will need to be employed to assess the impact and mitigations of this terrain.
column27:
The site has good access to the main road to the southern boarder of the plot, well connected to the town of Jokkmokk and it's highways to the rest of Sweden. 

In addition to main roads, there are also smaller tracks skirting the east and northern boundaries of the plot, highlighting the ease for mulitple entry points.

The geological reports from the site indicate the ground is mostly built up from glacial sedimentary deposits, presenting good drainage ground. In addition the site slopes towards the river to the north of the site (10m height difference from site to river), therefore run-off and drainage will naturally be in good order, removing the need for substantial drainage groundworks.

Otherwise Stelia have confirmed across all Norway sites that sufficient dark fibre connectivity for AI data centres can be implemented.

JC - Current State Summary
Owner of local fibre network: Nord-Salten Kraft Ltd (NSK)
Administered by: Trollfjord Bredbånd Ltd (TBB)
Backhaul provider: Altibox (via Signal Bredbånd)
Latency: ~15ms to Oslo
Bandwidth Available: Up to 10G (standard), scalable via DWDM
Redundancy risk: Limited; failover route via Narvik increases latency

column14:
Discussion opened with fibre provider Elisa

Questions asked: 
-What is the maximum dedicated bandwidth you can provide (e.g., 100
Gbps, 400 Gbps, 1 Tbps)?
-Do you offer dark fibre, wavelength services, or dedicated leased lines
between Välikangas and Helskinki, Kotka, sub-sea interconnectors?
-What are your latency metrics (ping times) from Välikangas to major hubs
(Helsinki, Stockholm, Amsterdam, Frankfurt)?

Answers:
-100G
-Yes, black fiber exists. WDM for wavelength services, and also dedicated leased lines. So
it’s a yes for every question.
-Latency (ping times) is 5-30ms.

Further guidance:
I received an answer from another fibre company, and they said that black fibre is available. column21:
Ranta-Luttila DC is in well positioned site from network connectivity perspective
● Site would need more investments on the fiber infrastructure, but building the infrastructure should be relatively easy
● Varkaus DC area has 3 diverse routes to the area:
○ two by Fingrid OPGW (optical fibre composite overhead ground wires), Dark fiber possibility
○ one by Telia terrestrial cable
○ one via VT5
● DNA and Elisa fiber infrastructures are not that close so additional investments would be required to get their networks connected to Ranta-Luttila
● There are no network core PoPs close to Varkaus DC so the operators would need to invest to DWDM and MPLS equipment at the site
● During discussions with the operators they indicated their willingness to invest in the fiber and equipment infra to the planned DC area

See additional info provided by MPY

A 7 MW power draw is possible to be implemented in the regional grid without addiitonal measures.

A new transformer will be installed to accommodate the remaining power draw above 7MW.

This assumes a number of investments in the distribution network, such as the establishment of a new feeder from the existing Skibotn substation – dedicated to our consumption. 

A suitable customer interface will be established at our connection point. 

The network infrastructure we develop within the distribution grid will be considered a customer-specific installation.
column9:
There is approximately 5,500 kVA of available capacity in the existing 22kV grid feeding the land plot.

For any further increase in capacity beyond this, it will be necessary to replace the main transformers at the Storbukt substation. However, space within the existing building is limited, and cannot accommodate significantly larger transformers. 

As a result, structural expansion of the facility would be required. 

Additionally, the existing 66 kV lines limit the total allowable load, and the maximum feasible transformer size is estimated at approximately 2 x 30 MVA. Upgrades to the distribution network between the Storbukt substation and the project site will also be necessary.

column13:
Option 1: Do Nothing - Land will go to public auction in 2026 and we could participate in the public process or use the backup land plot identified.

Option 2: Make a proposal to the municipality to reserve the land for us today. This proposal should include:
- That we continue the zoning process privately.
- That the purchase price would be 11.6 EUR - 23 EUR per square metre
- The site can be rented for up to 30 years at 7.5% of the purchase price with right to purchase at any time
- Consider whether we wish to pay for the Western Access Road
- Who would be our zoning consultant
- Who would be our legal counsel
- What kind of data centre
- Who will be the owners
- What activities will take place at the data centre
- How much employment will be created
- Any other considerations

We should engage a zoning consultant prior to the proposal to understand the zoning cost and for additional guidance on the land price proposal. One has been identified

The proposal would then be reviwewed by the city board and then if improved is reffered to the city council for final decision. Once reaching city council this becomes a political decision and public process.
